mnemonics.title: HERMITE Points & Tangents
mnemonics.content: HERMITE = 'H'eads (P0) and 'E'ndpoints (P1), 'R'ising 'M'otion 'I'n 'T'angents 'E'verywhere - Remember Hermite curves are defined by two endpoints and their tangent vectors (directions at endpoints).

mnemonics.title: Bezier Control Handles
mnemonics.content: Bezier = 'B'ind 'E'very 'Z'igzag 'I'n 'E'legant 'R'egions - Think of control points as invisible 'handles' that pull the curve toward them, but the curve doesn't necessarily pass through them (except endpoints).

mnemonics.title: Cubic Curve Cubes
mnemonics.content: Cubic = 'C'urves 'U'sing 'B'asis 'I'n 'C'ombinations - Cubic curves (degree 3) use 4 control points in Bezier or 2 points + 2 tangents in Hermite. Think '4 points make a cubic, think of a 3D cube (4 corners in each dimension).'

mnemonics.title: Bernstein Basis Blend
mnemonics.content: Bernstein = 'B'lend 'E'ach 'R'esult 'N'ear 'S'uccessive 'T'angents 'E'verywhere 'I'n 'N'ature - Bernstein polynomials are the basis functions that blend the control points together.

acronyms.usage: Used in both Hermite and Bezier to define the start and end points of the curve. In Hermite, the curve passes through these points. In Bezier, the curve starts at P0 and ends at P1.
Used in Hermite curves to define the slope/direction at the start (T0) and end (T1) points. They control how the curve leaves and enters the endpoints.
The parametric equation for a Bezier curve, where t ranges from 0 to 1. B(0) = P0 and B(1) = P1.
The parametric equation for a Hermite curve. Combines point and tangent information using blending functions.
Types of continuity between curve segments. C0 = they touch, C1 = smooth tangent (no sharp corner), C2 = smooth curvature change. Important for joining Bezier/Hermite segments smoothly.

acronyms.expansion: Basic Linear Interpolation of End points and Derivatives
acronyms.usage: The four blending functions in Hermite curves that combine P0, P1, T0, T1 to create the final curve.

visualTips.concept: Parametric Curve as a Moving Point
visualTips.visualization: Imagine a ball rolling along a track from point A to point B. The parameter 't' represents time - at t=0 you're at the start, at t=1 you're at the end. The curve is the path traced by the ball over time.

Think of Bezier control points as invisible magnets. The curve is a rubber band that gets pulled toward each control point. P0 and P1 have the strongest pull (the curve MUST pass through them), while P2 and P3 pull the curve toward them but the curve doesn't touch them.

At each endpoint, imagine a car steering wheel. The tangent vector (T0, T1) shows which direction the 'car' (curve) is heading when it leaves/enters that point. A longer tangent = more influence, like pressing the gas pedal.

Picture 4 people holding a blanket at its corners (control points). The Bernstein basis function for each point tells how much that corner 'lifts' the blanket at any given position. At t=0, only P0 lifts; at t=0.5, all contribute; at t=1, only P1 lifts.

visualTips.concept: Cubic Curves as Roller Coaster Hills
visualTips.visualization: A cubic (degree 3) curve can have at most one 'hill' or 'valley' - think of a roller coaster with one big dip. This is because cubic curves have 2 inflection points maximum. Higher degrees = more complex shapes.

analogies.analogy: Think of drawing a curve by specifying: where to start (P0), where to end (P1), which direction to leave the start (T0 like a arrow pointing out), and which direction to arrive at the end (T1 like an arrow pointing in). It's like giving someone directions: 'Go from Point A, heading Northeast, and arrive at Point B coming from the South.'

Imagine a puppeteer controlling a marionette (the curve) using strings attached to invisible control points. The strings pull the curve in different directions. The endpoints (P0, P3) are where the puppet's hands are attached firmly, while middle points (P1, P2) are just strings pulling the path.

Think of making a fruit juice blend. At t=0, it's 100% apple juice (P0). At t=1, it's 100% orange juice (P3). At t=0.5, it's a mix. The blending functions determine how much of each 'ingredient' (control point) goes into the mix at each step along t.

Think of road segments: C0 continuity = two road pieces just touching (you feel a bump), C1 = smooth connection like a highway on-ramp (tangent matches), C2 = perfect racing track smoothness where even the curvature changes seamlessly (like a professional race track).

Imagine a rubber band stretched around all the control points (forming the convex hull). The Bezier curve will always lie INSIDE this rubber band boundary - it can never 'escape' outside. This is like a dog on a leash (the curve) that can only roam within the reach of its owner (the control points).
